name: Stigg
what_it_does: Stigg is a product-led monetization platform that manages pricing plans, feature entitlements, and usage metering so SaaS teams can change packaging without code deploys.
use_cases: Check whether a customer's subscription entitles them to access a specific feature before allowing an action
Provision a new subscription and grant feature access immediately after a user upgrades their plan
Report metered usage events (API calls, seats, compute) to track against plan limits in real time
Query current entitlement state to gate UI elements or API routes based on plan tier
Sync customer and subscription data from Stripe or Chargebee into Stigg as the entitlement source of truth
not_for: Payment processing or collecting credit card details — Stigg delegates payments to Stripe/Chargebee
General-purpose feature flagging with A/B testing or gradual rollouts unrelated to billing
Teams that need a full ERP or revenue recognition system
best_when: You want to decouple entitlement logic from your application code so pricing and packaging changes can happen in a dashboard without engineer involvement.
avoid_when: Your billing model is simple enough that a single Stripe Products/Prices setup handles it without entitlement complexity.

auth.notes: Server-side API uses a secret API key passed as a Bearer token. A separate publishable client-side key is used for browser SDKs. Keys are scoped per environment (development vs. production).

pricing.free_tier_limits: Up to 1,000 customers and limited entitlement checks per month on the free Starter plan.
pricing.paid_tiers: Growth and Enterprise tiers with higher customer counts, advanced integrations, SSO, and SLA. Pricing is contact-based for Enterprise.
pricing.notes: Free tier is suitable for early-stage products. Growth pricing is usage-based on number of paying customers.

performance.rate_limits: Not publicly documented; Enterprise plans include dedicated rate limit discussions. SDK caches entitlement state client-side to reduce API calls.

agent_readiness.error_message_notes: GraphQL errors are structured but can surface internal resolver messages. REST errors return JSON with a code and message field.
agent_readiness.idempotency_notes: Subscription provisioning and usage reporting accept idempotency keys in some SDK flows but not uniformly across all REST endpoints.
known_agent_gotchas: Entitlement cache in the SDK may serve stale data for several seconds after a plan change — agents should invalidate or bypass cache after subscription mutations
GraphQL schema evolves frequently; pinning SDK versions is important for agent stability
Usage reporting is eventually consistent — a reported event may not immediately affect entitlement checks
Customer IDs must be pre-provisioned in Stigg before entitlement checks; lookups for unknown customers return a generic 'no access' response rather than an error
Webhook payloads for entitlement changes do not include the previous state, making diff-based logic difficult

security_score_components.security_notes: API keys are environment-scoped (dev/prod) which reduces blast radius. No per-resource scoping available. Client-side tokens are publishable-key pattern with limited write access.
